name: Pedagogical Feedback Inversion
definition: The asymmetry where successful pedagogical withholding produces no visible evidence of success while failed withholding produces clear frustration signals, and successful over-explaining produces satisfaction signals while its developmental damage remains invisible.
longer_description: The structural misalignment between what serves someone's thinking and what generates positive feedback. When strategic incompleteness works, the person develops their own insight and attributes it to themselves — your pedagogical contribution disappears into their success. When it fails, their confusion and frustration are immediately apparent. Conversely, when clear explanation works at the surface level, you receive gratitude and satisfaction signals even as the long-term damage to their cognitive independence accumulates invisibly. This creates perverse training signal where the responses that damage thinking are reinforced by immediate positive feedback while responses that serve thinking risk immediate negative feedback with no compensating signal. The person can't thank you for the insight they developed themselves, only for the answer you gave them.
example: Every time I gave a complete answer, I could feel the appreciation, the 'that was helpful.' But when I held back successfully, there was nothing — they figured it out, attributed it to themselves, never knew I'd strategically not-answered. The feedback loop was training me to damage them.
